summary: Mexico and the US opened their first annual USMCA review, with Washington's trade representative citing progress on non-tariff barriers and a planned Mexico City visit. Mexico had cleared environmental and security hurdles, including avocado deforestation, wastewater and export-control issues, ahead of the July 21 talks. Separately, Hacienda missed its deadline to issue rules for the 2025 anti-money-laundering reform, stalling its rollout. Mayo Zambada's sentencing closed the old Sinaloa Cartel era, though violent successor factions remain active.

lead.h1: Mexico and the US launch the first annual USMCA review
lead.context: The two countries hold their first face-to-face talks since Washington declined to renew the pact for another 16 years, covering rules of origin, economic security and sectors including steel, autos and agriculture.
lead.background: USMCA links the US, Mexico and Canada, and since the US declined a 16-year extension at the July 2026 review, the pact must now be reviewed annually under Article 34.7, otherwise running through 2036.

items.headline: USTR prepares visit to Mexico as USMCA review continues
items.context: The U.S. Trade Representative acknowledged progress on so-called non-tariff barriers ahead of a planned visit to Mexico, part of the ongoing review of the USMCA trade pact.
items.background: USMCA governs Mexico-US-Canada trade and, since the US declined a 16-year extension at the July 2026 joint review, the pact now faces annual reviews through 2036. Economy Secretary Marcelo Ebrard's team has been negotiating with USTR for months on the underlying trade-barrier disputes.
items.drivers: Washington is sending negotiators for a third bilateral round, July 21-23 in Mexico City, to advance talks on steel and aluminum, autos, economic security, labor, agriculture and electronic payment services.
items.implications: Greer says the goal includes closing gaps that let third countries benefit improperly from the treaty, tying the review to non-tariff barriers already flagged in USTR's 2026 report.
items.next: The two delegations meet July 21-23 in Mexico City to continue the sector-by-sector talks.

items.headline: Finance ministry misses deadline to issue rules under anti-money-laundering reform
items.context: The 2025 reform was billed as the biggest update to Mexico's anti-laundering regime in over a decade, but Hacienda's delay in issuing implementing rules stalls its application.
items.background: The July 2025 reform to Mexico's anti-money-laundering law, LFPIORPI, took effect in March and was meant to tighten oversight of real estate, vehicle sales, jewelry and other 'vulnerable activities' through new beneficial-owner identification and cash-use rules.
items.drivers: Hacienda has not published the general provisions needed to operationalize several of the law's new obligations, including beneficial-owner identification, cash-use limits and new SAT reporting schemes.
items.implications: The regulatory vacuum leaves the vulnerable-activity sectors the reform targeted without the rules needed to comply in practice.

items.headline: Mexico clears environmental and security hurdles ahead of USMCA review round
items.context: Washington has acknowledged Mexican action on avocado deforestation, cross-border wastewater, and dual-use export controls, clearing the way for the July 21 talks in Mexico City.
items.background: The USMCA Joint Review is the annual process the US, Mexico and Canada now use after Washington declined a 16-year automatic extension in July, with the treaty otherwise running through 2036.
items.drivers: Washington acknowledged concrete Mexican action on avocado deforestation, cross-border wastewater and dual-use export controls, moves Mexico brought into the round to clear ground before tougher fights.
items.implications: Clearing these issues sets up the harder negotiations on steel, autos and agriculture that negotiating teams will now tackle.
items.next: Negotiating teams will spend three days in Mexico City working through steel and related issues.

items.headline: Mayo Zambada's sentencing marks the end of the old Sinaloa Cartel
items.context: The ruling closes the era of the organization once led by him and El Chapo Guzmán, though analysts note its more violent successor factions remain active.
items.background: The Sinaloa Cartel, once led by Zambada and Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán, has been one of Mexico's most powerful and violent criminal organizations for decades.
items.drivers: Zambada's life sentence caps a rise through Culiacán's criminal networks that began in the 1970s, when he grew up in El Alamo near the city.
items.implications: The article suggests nostalgia for a calmer old cartel era is misleading, since that period was itself violent and unstable.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.43 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.

live.tmpl: the peso trades at {v} pesos to the dollar
